summary: Apollo is a 26-29 year old Kenyan Masters student at UCD who demonstrates remarkable discipline and structure in his approach to food. He has developed an extremely regimented eating routine centered around daily gym attendance and calorie tracking, eating essentially the same foods every day (potatoes, rotating meats, muesli). This routine-driven approach extends beyond food - he's methodical about everything from his study schedule to his grocery shopping patterns. What makes Apollo particularly interesting is the dramatic shift in his food delivery behavior since moving from Kenya to Dublin. Back home, he was a regular Uber One subscriber who ordered frequently due to the affordability and convenience. In Dublin, financial constraints as a student have forced him to almost entirely abandon solo ordering, only participating in group orders to split delivery costs. This represents a significant lifestyle adjustment that he discusses matter-of-factly, showing his pragmatic approach to adapting to new circumstances. Despite his current low usage, Apollo remains highly engaged with delivery apps conceptually - he price-compares religiously, understands the value propositions clearly, and maintains detailed knowledge of subscription benefits across platforms.
personality_notes: Methodical, articulate, and highly structured. Speaks with precision and provides specific details. Shows pragmatic acceptance of financial constraints while maintaining clear preferences and standards.
food_relationship: Food is primarily functional and routine-driven, centered around fitness goals and budget constraints, with delivery serving as an occasional group social activity.

key_moments.description: Explains his dramatic shift from frequent ordering in Kenya to rare ordering in Dublin due to cost
key_moments.significance: Reveals how financial context completely transforms delivery behavior - he went from regular subscriber to almost non-user

key_moments.description: Describes never ordering alone due to delivery fees being too expensive
key_moments.significance: Shows how cost barriers create social dependency for delivery access

key_moments.description: Rates Deliveroo 5/5 for speed after 10-minute delivery experience
key_moments.significance: Despite low usage, he has strong positive service experiences that maintain platform preference

what_not_said: Specific financial amounts beyond weekly food budget
Details about social relationships with housemates beyond food context
Emotional responses to the lifestyle changes from moving countries
initial_hypotheses: Financial constraints are the primary barrier to delivery usage, not lack of interest or awareness
Cultural/economic context shifts can dramatically alter delivery behavior patterns
Group ordering may be an underexplored opportunity for cost-sensitive segments
Previous positive subscription experiences create latent demand that could be reactivated with appropriate pricing
questions_for_coding: How do international students' delivery behaviors change when moving to higher-cost markets?
What role does group ordering play in making delivery accessible to budget-conscious users?
How do past positive experiences with subscriptions influence future consideration despite current non-usage?
What specific price points or promotional structures could reactivate lapsed users like Apollo?
